allergens: gluten (wheat in soy sauce and Marmite)

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Marmite Pork Ribs is approximately 300 g, comprising several bone-in pork rib pieces coated in a sticky Marmite-based glaze, served as a shared zi char dish.

nutrient_source.title: Pork ribs, cooked, braised — adapted with Marmite glaze adjustment; reference base: USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy, Pork, fresh, loin, blade (chops), bone-in, cooked, braised (NDB 10006); Marmite glaze contribution estimated from IMR Malaysia MyFCD condiment data
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service / IMR Malaysia MyFCD
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Base nutrient values derived from USDA SR Legacy braised pork rib data (NDB 10006, approximately 263 kcal/100g cooked). Marmite glaze adds approximately 15–20 kcal/100g from sugar and yeast extract, sodium contribution from Marmite and soy sauce estimated at +180 mg/100g above base. Final per-100g values represent a composite estimate for the finished dish. No exact match found in HPB Singapore Foodo or IMR Malaysia MyFCD for this specific preparation.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of mixed meat and sauce dishes — analogous estimate based on braised pork with sweet sauce; reference: Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International Tables of Glycemic Index and Glycemic Load Values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care peer-reviewed journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Marmite Pork Ribs. GI estimated at 35 based on analogous braised pork with sweet glaze dishes. The dish is predominantly protein and fat with moderate sugar from the Marmite glaze; GI is driven by the sugar/starch component of the sauce. Glycemic load per serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (35 × 25.5) / 100 = 8.9, where carbohydrate per 300g serving = 8.5 × 3 = 25.5g.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content from Marmite yeast extract, soy sauce, and oyster sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid Marmite Pork Ribs due to its very high sodium content of approximately 620 mg per 100g (approximately 1860 mg per restaurant serving), which represents over 80% of the recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate sugar content from Marmite glaze and low-to-moderate glycemic index
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume Marmite Pork Ribs in small portions given its low GI of approximately 35, but should be mindful of the moderate sugar content (approximately 5.5g per 100g) and the high fat content, which can contribute to insulin resistance and overall caloric excess.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High saturated fat, high sodium, and significant cholesterol content
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume Marmite Pork Ribs only occasionally and in small portions, as the combination of high saturated fat (approximately 6.2g/100g), high sodium (approximately 620 mg/100g), and significant cholesterol (approximately 78 mg/100g) poses compounded cardiovascular risk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Marmite Pork Ribs is a popular Malaysian and Singaporean zi char (Chinese-style hawker stir-fry) dish consisting of bone-in pork ribs that are first deep-fried until golden and crispy, then tossed or braised in a rich, sticky glaze made from Marmite yeast extract, soy sauce, oyster sauce, sugar, and garlic. The result is a deeply savoury, slightly sweet, and intensely umami-flavoured dish with a caramelised, glossy coating over tender, juicy pork meat. The texture contrasts a lightly crisp exterior with succulent, fall-off-the-bone meat. Marmite, a British yeast extract paste, was adopted into Southeast Asian cooking and became a signature flavouring in Malaysian and Singaporean tze char restaurants. The dish is typically served as a shared main course alongside steamed white rice and other zi char dishes. Nutritionally, Marmite Pork Ribs is calorie-dense at approximately 280 kcal per 100g, with a high protein content of approximately 22.5g and significant fat at 17g per 100g, including 6.2g saturated fat. The sodium content is very high at approximately 620 mg per 100g, making it a concern for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The glycemic index is estimated at approximately 35 (low), driven by the moderate sugar content of the glaze. The dish is not suitable for halal diets due to the use of pork.

confidence_reason: No exact match for Marmite Pork Ribs was found in HPB Singapore Foodo, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or USDA FoodData Central. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA SR Legacy braised pork rib data with estimated adjustments for the Marmite glaze. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is medium rather than low because the base pork rib data is from a high-quality approved source and the glaze adjustments are conservative and well-documented.
notes: Vitamin D (vitamin_d_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this preparation. Vitamin E (vitamin_e_mg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this preparation. Selenium (selenium_mcg) is set to -1 as it is not reported in the USDA SR Legacy source for this preparation. Monounsaturated fat, polyunsaturated fat, trans fat, potassium, calcium, iron, cholesterol, vitamins, phosphorus, magnesium, and zinc values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork rib data and may vary depending on the specific cut and preparation method used by individual restaurants. The Marmite glaze contributes significant sodium; actual sodium may vary by recipe. This dish contains pork and is not suitable for halal or kosher diets. The cuisine_tags in the input JSON (Seafood Dish, Crab, Beer) appear to be restaurant-level tags for the source restaurant (George Town Tze Char And Craft Beer) rather than dish-specific tags and have not been carried forward to this record.
